Remember, being chartists, we believe that all of ACIC's fundamentals have already been baked into its technical charts. Therefore, let's start off with ACIC's long-term chart to see why the supply/demand paradigm has turned bearish this year and whether current trends have any chance of turning around any time soon.

20-Year Technicals

There are three trends that we take note of in ACIC's 20-year chart, with all three being concerning in nature. Firstly, shares topped out in December of last year at a resistance level that was predefined in many ways between 2015 and 2019. Secondly, when shares topped out late last year, the long-term trend-following indicator (ADX) began to drop off at a level well above 50 and has been falling through the duration of calendar 2025. It remains, though, at an elevated level (Approximately 41), which means consolidation in the stock price is probably the best-case scenario here for ACIC as we head into 2026.

The third and probably most concerning technical trend on ACIC's long-term chart is the crossing over of the MACD indicator, which took place earlier this year. Although not a death sentence in itself, as the 10-month moving average has been holding steady, the fact that the crossover took place well above the zero-line demonstrates that shares remain overvalued from a long-term technical standpoint. Remember, the MACD is a potent tool on long-term charts due to the sizable amount of info that goes into the makeup of the signals and the fact that it plays a dual role (momentum & trend) over time in its readouts, all things remaining equal.

Intermediate Technical Chart Strength Due To Bullish Q2 Earnings

When we pull up an intermediate 7-year chart, we see that recent share-price strength has resulted in a bullish crossover of the weekly MACD, presenting a potential intermediate buy signal. However, given the trends we discussed earlier on the 20-year chart, we would caution investors to 'go on' shorter-term charts for the following reasons.

Ultimately, the technical charts always align with the fundamentals of the respective company. To this point, we acknowledge that ACIC stock has experienced strength over the past couple of months, largely due to a solid Q2 earnings release (released on August 6, 2025), where both GAAP earnings of $0.53 per share and revenues of $86.47 million exceeded estimates by sizable margins.

Q3 Cautious Outlook

However, despite a 26% year-over-year increase in revenues and a 51% growth in pre-tax earnings in the quarter, management took a cautious approach with respect to Q3 (Ending 9/30/2025), where earnings are expected to be announced on 11/6/2025. Management tried to reassure investors that growth would likely resume in Q4 after the hurricane season, but it remains too early to tell whether the underwriting environment will change for the better in the fourth quarter. Furthermore, it should be pointed out that the prominence of insider selling in recent months does nothing for investor confidence as we approach the latter quarters of the year.

Suffice it to say, one can not simply divulge the recent Q2 earnings report but rather step back and take a broader view with respect to ACIC's growth path. The bottom-line expectation for Q3 is normalized earnings of $0.39 per share, which is a number that compares very poorly with Q3 in 2024 ($0.58 per share). Furthermore, the present bottom-line expectation for fiscal 2025 is approximately $1.35 per share, again, a number which is well down from what ACIC earned in preceding years.

Therefore, when one takes into account the concerning technicals above, negative growth rates and ACIC's trailing P/B of 1.95 (which comes in well above the company's 5-year average & sector median), it stands to reason why upside potential in the stock seems limited at this time.

Management Remains Reluctant To Take More Risk

Bulls may point to ACIC's Q2 combined ratio of 60%, which demonstrated strong profitability, but the company's target combined ratio remains at 65% which prompts the following question. How can ACIC grow its revenues and earnings if it is not willing to take more risk? We state this because, considering the company's stated valuation (book value per share of approximately $6), the company is not making enough money (poor growth rates) to justify the present valuation.

This brings us back to the initial 20-year chart above and the fact that many long-term investors in ACIC (Especially those who invested between 2014 & 2018) have unrealized losses in their portfolios, especially when real gains instead of nominal gains are taken into account. The absence of 'predictability' when it comes to recurring income potential and the ever-changing dynamics of the markets that ACIC serves acts as a major headwind to the long-term investor.

At present, for example, with climate change continuing to wreak havoc and with property insurance rates on the slide in most markets recently, management's problem is knowing when to risk capital or when to keep underwriting at a minimum, at least until the proverbial 'storm' passes by. This increase in 'risk appetite,' however, is what investors ultimately will demand, which is why market dynamics you feel will have to shift somewhat for ACIC stock to have any chance of trading meaningfully higher.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, although there are clear warning signals in ACIC stock, we are going to reiterate our 'Hold' rating as shares continue to trade well ahead of their 10-month moving average ($11.42 per share). Share-price consolidation seems the best-case scenario here unless market dynamics can shift meaningfully to prompt much better growth rates. We look forward to continued coverage.
